coredata.dc:title: Chiral carbonized polymer dots simultaneously achieving solid state luminescence and circularly polarized luminescence
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yijie
Yu, Linbo
Lv, Tingjie
Wang, Jing
Zhou, Shengju
Sun, Xiaofeng
coredata.dc:description: Circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) materials are a hot research in the field of chiral luminescent materials. Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) have attracted more and more attention due to their tunable photoluminescence, high photostability, low cost and excellent biocompatibility. These outstanding properties of CPDs make them have bright prospects in the field of CPL. However, due to the aggregation-caused quenching of CPDs, it is difficult to simultaneously achieve solid state luminescence and CPL of solid CPDs itself. In this work, we have employed p-phenyldiacetonitrile and terephthalaldehyde/isophthalaldehyde as carbon source precursors, L-/D-tryptophan as chiral precursors to prepare chiral CPDs with different luminescence ,colors which can simultaneously achieve solid state luminescence and CPL of solid CPDs itself for the first time.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral
Carbonized polymer dots
Multicolor
Solid state luminescence
Circularly polarized luminescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM images of Y-L-CPDs (a), Y-D-CPDs (b), R-L-CPDs (c), R-D-CPDs (d). XRD spectra (e), Raman spectra (f), FT-IR spectra (g) and XPS survey spectra (h) of four CPDs. High-resolution XPS O1s spectra of L-R-CPDs (i), D-R-CPDs (j), L-Y-CPDs (k), and D-Y-CPDs (l).

(a) The PL emission spectra of Y-L-CPDs, Y-D-CPDs, R-L-CPDs and R-D-CPDs under 365 nm excitation. (b, c) The PL emission spectra of Y-L-CPDs (b) and R-L-CPDs (c) at different excitation wavelengths. (d) The excitation spectra of Y-L-CPDs, Y-D-CPDs, R-L-CPDs and R-D- CPDs. (e) The UV–vis absorption spectra of Y-L-CPDs, Y-D-CPDs, R-L-CPDs and R-D-CPDs. (f) Lifetime decay curves of L-R-CPDs, D-R-CPDs, L-Y-CPDs and D-Y-CPDs in solid state.
CD and the corresponding absorption spectra of (a) Y-CPDs and R-CPDs (b) in solid state. CPL and the corresponding emission spectra of Y-CPDs (c) and R-CPDs (d) in solid state.
Scheme illustrating the synthesis of chiral CPDs using phenyldiacetonitrile, terephthalaldehyde/isophthalaldehyde and L-/D-tryptophan as precursors.

head.ce:title: Chiral carbonized polymer dots simultaneously achieving solid state luminescence and circularly polarized luminescence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo, 255000, Shandong, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255000, Shandong, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) materials are a hot research in the field of chiral luminescent materials. Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) have attracted more and more attention due to their tunable photoluminescence, high photostability, low cost and excellent biocompatibility. These outstanding properties of CPDs make them have bright prospects in the field of CPL. However, due to the aggregation-caused quenching of CPDs, it is difficult to simultaneously achieve solid state luminescence and CPL of solid CPDs itself. In this work, we have employed p-phenyldiacetonitrile and terephthalaldehyde/isophthalaldehyde as carbon source precursors, L-/D-tryptophan as chiral precursors to prepare chiral CPDs with different luminescence ,colors which can simultaneously achieve solid state luminescence and CPL of solid CPDs itself for the first time.

The prepared chiral carbonized polymer dots can simultaneously achieve solid state luminescence and circularly polarized luminescence.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality exists widely in nature and is a basic property of nature, which represents an important symmetrical feature [1–3]. The study of chirality not only inspires our understanding of life on earth and the origin of the universe, but also has great significance in the fields of life science and materials science [4,5]. Circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) is a phenomenon that chiral luminescent materials emit different left-handed and right-handed circularly polarized light in the excited state, which is an important means to understand chirality [6,7]. In recent years, CPL materials have received great attention owing to their widespread application in the fields of 3D display, information storage, biological probes, optoelectronic devices, and photocatalytic asymmetric synthesis [8–10].

With the emergence of nanomaterials, the chirality of nanoscale has become a research frontier in the field of chiral materials due to its unique physical and chemical properties and wide application [11–13]. These nanoparticles include semiconductor quantum dots [14,15], perovskite [16], metal-organic framework [17,18], metal nanoclusters [19,20], and CPDs [21,22]. Among these materials, CPDs, as a new type of carbon nanomaterials, have attracted more and more attention due to their tunable photoluminescence, high photostability, low cost and excellent biocompatibility [23,24]. Nowadays, great progress has been made in the research of chiral CPDs prepared from chiral precursors, which show obvious circular dichroism (CD) due to retention of the chiral structure [25,26]. However, the CPL of chiral CPDs is not easily expressed, and some chiral CPDs cannot detect the CPL signal [22]. How to realize the CPL properties of CPDs has become one of the research focuses nowadays.

There are two main strategies for achieving the CPL properties of CPDs. (1) The CPDs as a fluorescent guest are co-assembled with a chiral template host such as cellulose nanocrystals [27] or perovskite nanocrystal [16] or amino acid-derived gels [28] or G-quadruplex DNA [29], achieving CPL properties through the transfer and induction of chirality [30]. (2) Another more direct route is to achieve intrinsic CPL properties of chiral CPDs employing the atropisomers [31] or cholesterol derivatives [32] or enantiomers of amino acids [33] as a chiral precursor. Kumar et al. [33] firstly reported chiral CPDs with CPL properties in solution state, and realized solid CPL of CPDs with the help of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA). However, CPL emission of solid CPDs itself is not realized, and the emission wavelength is only limited to blue. Most of CPDs in solid state suffer from serious aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) due to direct π-π interactions and fluorescence resonance energy transfer [34]. Therefore, it is still a huge challenge to simultaneously achieve solid state luminescence and CPL of solid CPDs itself.

Here, we employed p-phenyldiacetonitrile and terephthalaldehyde/isophthalaldehyde as carbon source precursors, L-/D-tryptophan as chiral precursors to prepare chiral CPDs simultaneously achieving solid state luminescence and CPL. Among amino acids, tryptophan has a higher melting point (281 °C), lower than the reaction temperature (140 °C), which is beneficial for preserving the chiral structure [28]. As shown in Scheme 1 and Fig. S1a, we obtained the yellow chiral CPDs (Y-L-CPDs and Y-D-CPDs) using isophthalaldehyde as the precursor and the red chiral CPDs (R-L-CPDs and R-D-CPDs) using terephthalaldehyde as the precursor. However, when the precursor is o-phthalaldehyde, the reaction product appears obvious liquid-solid stratification. The liquid is black and no fluorescence (Fig. S1b), the solid shows yellow fluorescence, but contains a lot of black impurities (Fig. S1a), which may be caused by excessive carbonization due to the low melting point of o-phthalaldehyde (∼55 °C), so we abandon this product.

Terephthalaldehyd, isophthalaldehyde, o-Phthalaldehyde, NaOH, p-phenyldiacetonitrile, acetone, l-tryptophan, d-tryptophan were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. and used as received. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ cm prepared from a UPH-IV ultrapure water purifier (China) was used throughout the experimental process.

Fluorescence spectra were tested on a LS-55 spectrofluorometer. Fluorescence lifetimes were performed on an Edinburgh Instruments FLS980 (Edinburgh Instruments LTD). The absorption spectra were collected by a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer. The high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were recorded on JEOL 2100 system. Raman spectra were collected by laser micro-Raman spectroscopy. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by a PANalytical X'Pert Powder diffractometer (PANalytical, Holland) with Cu Kα radiation. The FT-IR spectra were measured by a VERTEX-70/70v FT-IR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250). The fluorescence quantum yields were measured by Hamamatsu absolute quantum yield measuring instrument Quantaurus-QY. The 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra were characterized by Bruker Avance 400 NMR instrument. Electrospray-mass (ESI-MS) spectra were obtained from Thermo Scientific QE plus. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out on TGA 5500 under a N2 atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 °C·min−1.

For yellow chiral CPDs (Y-L-CPDs and Y-D-CPDs), the isophthalaldehyde (1.56 g), p-phenyldiacetonitrile (1.34 g), L-/D-tryptophan (0.176 g) and NaOH (0.3 g) were dissolved in 10 mL water. After stirring for 30 min and ultrasonication for 10 min, the precursor solution was transferred to a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 140 °C for 1.5 h. After the reaction, the autoclave were cooled to room temperature naturally. Then, excess acetone was added to precipitate the product and the supernatant was removed. The precipitate was washed thrice with acetone and then washed thrice with water. After heating, the Y-L-CPDs and Y-D-CPDs were obtained. For red chiral CPDs (R-L-CPDs and R-D- CPDs), the isophthalaldehyde was replaced with terephthalaldehyd (1.56 g), and other conditions remained unchanged.

In order to explore the reasons for the different fluorescence colors, the structure of the four CPDs were characterized. The morphology of the four CPDs is observed with a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a–d, the HR-TEM, shows that the particle sizes of the four CPDs are evenly distributed, and the average particle sizes of Y-L-CPDs, Y-D-CPDs R-L-CPDs and R-D-CPDs, are 3.7 nm, 3.1 nm, 3.8 nm and 3.4 nm, respectively. There is no significant differences in the particle size of four CPDs, which indicates that the color change is not caused by quantum effects. All the CPDs have lattice fringes with lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (inset images in Fig. 1a–d), corresponding to the (100) lattice planes of graphite [35]. In the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. 1e), the Y-CPDs show a clear broad peak pattern, indicating the presence of an amorphous carbon structure. While the R-CPDs show a distinct sharp peak, probably because the structure formed by terephthalaldehyde and p-phenyldiacetonitrile is more ordered after a continuous polymerization and carbonization process [36,37]. In the Raman spectra (Fig. 1f), it is found that there are D and G bands at around 1380 and 1600 cm−1 of four CPDs. We usually use the area ratio of G peak and D peak (IG/ID) to evaluate the degree of graphitization of CPDs. The IG/ID values of Y-L-CPDs, Y-D-CPDs, R-L-CPDs and R-D-CPDs are 3.42, 3.05, 5.03, and 4.94, respectively, indicating that the R-CPDs have a higher degree of graphitization than the Y-CPDs [38]. The higher degree of graphitization and sp2 domain induce the decrease of band gap, which leads to the redshift of emission wavelength [39,40]. The thermostability of the CPDs was characterized by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). As shown in Fig. S2, the weight loss before 100 °C could be ascribed to water of crystallization. The weight loss after 230 °C is ascribed to species that are linked to the CPDs via covalent bonds or some active parts of the carbon cores [28]. It is worth noting that the decomposition curve of the R-L-CPDs is above the Y-L-CPDs after 230 °C, which indicates that the thermal stability of the R-L-CPDs is greater than that of the Y-L-CPDs.

Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR), elemental analysis and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) are employed to explore the role of functional groups in changing the emission wavelength of CPDs. In the FT-IR (Fig. 1g), the wide stretching vibration at 3100 cm−1 to 3600 cm−1 indicates the presence of N-H/O-H [41]. The stretching vibration band at 2214 cm−1 is assigned to the residual CN of p-phenyldiacetonitrile. The peaks 1686 cm−1 and 1260 cm−1 represent CO/CN stretching vibration and C-O stretching vibration, respectively. The peaks at 1520 cm−1 correspond to CC stretching vibration [28,42]. In Table S1, elemental analysis show that the nitrogen content of the CPDs of the two colors is not much difference, which indicates the color change may not be caused by the nitrogen content. While the oxygen content of R-CPDs is higher than that of Y-CPDs, which indicate that the wavelength redshift may be a result of the increase in the degree of surface oxidation. XPS results show that three peaks are observed at 284, 400 and 531 eV, representing C1s, N1s, O1s, respectively (Fig. 1h). The high-resolution C1s, N1s and O1s spectra of four CPDs are fitted. C1s spectra can be divided into three peaks: C-C/CC (284.7 eV), C-N/C-O (286.1 eV) and CO/CN (288.5eV) (Figs. S3a–d) [43]. N1s consists of three components, including the presence of pyridine N (399 eV), pyrrole N/CN (399.5 eV), and N-H (400.1 eV) (Figs. S4a–d) [42,44]. O1s spectra show the presence of CO (531.8 eV) and C-O (533.4 eV) (Fig. 1i-l) [28]. From Y-CPDs to R-CPDs, the content of CO increases significantly, while the content of C-OH/C-O-C decreases (Table S2). In the 1H NMR spectra (Fig. S5), the peaks at 0–3 ppm are the chemical shift of the alkyl region. The peaks at 3–7 ppm are attributed to the amino and double bond regions. The presence of the -NH2 proton at 5.75 ppm indicates that the primary amine is introduced into the heterocyclic surface [45]. It can be clearly seen that the double bond content of the Y-L-CPDs is lower than that of the R-L-CPDs, which is also consistent with the conclusion of the Raman spectra. The aromatic ring signals in the 7–9 ppm attributed to the proton resonance of the graphitized carbon core show a significant abundance of R-L-CPDs compared to Y-L-CPDs. More than 10 ppm can be assigned to aldehyde and carboxyl groups. The multiple peaks of ESI-MS spectra demonstrate the polydispersity of CPDs (Fig. S6). This may be due to in the polymerization-carbonization process, the number of molecules participating in the polymerization-carbonization is random, and the molecular weight of various CPDs is definitely different [46]. Therefore, it can be concluded that the color change of CPDs may be caused by the high degree of graphitization and sp2 hybridization, as well as the increase in the degree of surface oxidation [47].

The optical properties of the four CPDs are characterized. As shown in the (Fig. 2 a), under 365 nm excitation, the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of Y-L- CPDs, Y-D-CPDs, R-L-CPDs and R-D-CPDs are 532, 536, 599 and 601 nm, respectively. Then, the PL emission spectra of the four CPDs were tested at different excitation wavelengths. As shown in the Fig. 2b, c and Figs. S7 and S8, the four CPDs all have a certain excitation dependence. The excitation spectra (Fig. 2d) show that the best excitation peaks of the R-CPDs are at 370 nm, while the Y-CPDs have two best excitation peaks at 370 nm and 435 nm. The absolute fluorescence quantum yields of the powders of Y-L-CDs, Y-D-CDs, R-L-CDs and R-D-CDs at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm are 5.4%, 6.1%, 7.6% and 6.9%, respectively. In the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 2e), the four CPDs have strong absorption peaks at around 315 nm and 385 nm, which are attributed to the n-π* transitions of CPDs [48,49]. Besides, it is found that the second absorption peak in R-CPDs is wider than that in Y-CPDs. This may be because the R-CPDs have more CO/CN, which is consistent with the results of XPS. The fluorescence lifetime test shows double exponential attenuation as shown in the Fig. 2f. The average fluorescence lifetimes of Y-L-CPDs, Y-D-CPDs, R-L-CPDs, and R-D-CPDs, are 11.15, 12.39, 6.88 and 6.54 ns, respectively (Table S3). The fluorescence lifetime of R-CPDs is lower than that of Y-CPDs, which indicates the excited molecules of R-CPDs have more non-radiative transitions [50,51].

Finally, the chirality of the ground state and the chirality of the excited state of the four solid CPDs were studied by circular dichroism (CD) and CPL spectra. In the CD spectra of Y-CPDs (Fig. 3 a), the mirror CD signal with positive sign of Y-L-CPDs and negative sign of D-Y-CPDs with three peaks of 217 nm, 358 nm and 468 nm confirm the formation of chiral CPDs. Similarly, in the CD spectra of R-CPDs (Fig. 3b), the mirror CD signal with negative sign of R-L-CPDs and positive sign of R-D-CPDs with two peaks at 215 nm and 456 nm also confirm the formation of chiral CPDs. The CD signals of the four solid CPDs are completely different from the CD signals of the chiral additive tryptophan, which indicates that the CD signals of CPDs are not directly contributed by the precursor. Tryptophan has an obvious symmetric peak before 250 nm. Therefore, the CD signal of the CPDs near 215 nm is inherited from the intrinsic chiral chromophore of the precursor L-/D-tryptophan. In addition to inheriting the chiral groups from the chiral precursor, the new CD signals are generated after carbonization and polymerization. All the new CD signals are located after 300 nm, which may be attributed to the n-π* transition in the aromatic sp2 domain in the chiral environment. The CPL spectra exhibit very nice symmetric peaks at the corresponding emission wavelength of 540 nm for Y-CPDs and 600 nm for R-CPDs (Fig. 3c and d), which proves that the four CPDs has excellent excited state chirality. The CD measures the chirality of the sample at the ground state, while the CPL measures the chirality of the sample at the excited state [6]. The signs of CD signals of R-L(D)-CDs are the same as those of their CPL signals (Fig. 3b, d), which indicates that the structures of the ground and excited states are similar. However, the signs of CD and CPL signals of Y-L(D)-CDs are opposite (Fig. 3a, c), which may be due to the equilibrium structure of the electronically excited state is different from the ground state geometry [52]. The dissymmetric factor glum = 2(IL−IR)/(IL + IR) was used to quantify the degree of chiral asymmetry of CPDs, where IL and IR are left-handed and right-handed circularly polarized light, respectively [7,33]. The glum of Y-CPDs and R-CPDs are 1.4 × 10−3 and 2.0 × 10−4, respectively. We compared some reported CPD-based CPL materials and made a table for easy comparison (Table S4). Although the glum of intrinsic CPL of chiral CPDs is lower than that of CPDs co-assembled with a chiral template (Table S4), the good stability and processability make the direct route achieved intrinsic CPL properties of chiral CDs is a promising technique without the need for a chiral matrix [31]. More importantly, as far as we know, this is the first time to realize CPL signal emission in solid CPDs itself.

In conclusion, we prepared the yellow chiral CPDs and red chiral CPDs using p-phenyldiacetonitrile and terephthalaldehyde/isophthalaldehyde as carbon source precursors, L-/D-tryptophan as chiral precursors. The structural analysis indicates that the color change of CPDs may be caused by the high degree of graphitization and sp2 hybridization, as well as the increase in the degree of surface oxidation. More importantly, the chiral CPDs can simultaneously achieve solid state luminescence and CPL of solid CPDs itself. The work will open new avenues for the investigation of excited stated chirality of CPDs in the solid state.

Yijie Wang: Investigation, Conducted the experiments, Writing – original draft. Linbo Yu: Investigation, Data curation. Tingjie Lv: Investigation, Data curation. Jing Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Shengju Zhou: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Xiaofeng Sun: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22002075) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR202211230226).
